persons.arguments_against: Strongest case against: Langley is a common surname in this corpus (15 officials, 5 biographies), the stint name is initials-only ('C. G.'), and the honour check failed (the staff lists never print the K.C.), so this could be a different C. G. Langley — perhaps a relative — in the Caribbean service. This fails because the link rests on a unique, senior office with exact place and date agreement: the biography reads 'atty.-gen., Leeward Islands, 1931' with a 'dual appt. atty.-gen. and ag. col. sec., 1934-36', and the stint is the Attorney-General of the Leeward Islands listed 1932-1937, first appearing the edition-year after the 1931 appointment. There is only one Attorney-General of the colony at a time; two C. G. Langleys holding it in the same span is not a live hypothesis. The missing K.C. in the staff-list honours column is an omission pattern common in these tables, not contradictory evidence; the stint's end (1937) also coheres with the bio's move to 'adminstr., Antigua, 1936-38' and B. Guiana in 1937.

evidence_cited.fact: C. G. Langley | Attorney-General | Judicial Establishment
dual appt. atty.-gen. and ag. col. sec., 1934-36

persons.would_overturn: A second biography or record of a different C. G. Langley serving as a law officer in the Leeward Islands in the 1930s, or evidence that the Attorney-Generalship changed hands to another Langley within 1932-1937.

notes: Unique colony-level office (Attorney-General, Leeward Islands) with initials and chronology in lockstep: bio appointment 1931, staff listing 1932-1937, departure consistent with the bio's 1936-38 Antigua administratorship and 1937 British Guiana judgeship. 'CABLETON' is an OCR artifact (likely 'Carleton'). The auxiliary 'Chairman, Public Library' rows in 1932-33 are an ex officio role under the same name and colony, not a separate person.
